summary: Gruber analyzes the expected announcement of Apple's transition from Intel to ARM chips at WWDC 2020, arguing it's essentially a foregone conclusion since developers need lead time before ARM Macs ship in 2021. He draws extensive parallels to the 2005 PowerPC-to-Intel transition, noting that Apple's in-house chips already offer vastly superior performance-per-watt. He explores several open questions: whether Apple will offer x86 emulation, what developer transition hardware will look like (dismissing the iPad Pro idea), how virtualization and Boot Camp will be affected, and whether the Osborne Effect will hurt current Mac sales. He concludes that the transition will follow the same playbook as 2005, and that most consumers won't notice or care about the underlying chip change.
key_points: Apple must pre-announce the ARM transition at WWDC because developers need months of lead time to recompile apps, unlike Apple's usual preference for surprise announcements.
The performance-per-watt argument for ARM mirrors the 2005 Intel transition almost exactly — a $400 iPhone SE already outperforms a $3,000 MacBook Pro in single-core benchmarks.
Apple will likely transition the entire Mac lineup to ARM, including pro desktops, because the logic of switching demands full commitment.
iPad Pros running macOS as developer transition kits is implausible — RAM limitations and Apple's philosophical separation of iPad and Mac make it a non-starter.
The Osborne Effect on current Intel Mac sales will be minimal because most consumers don't understand or care about the difference between chip architectures.
money_quotes: They already make a $400 iPhone that out-benchmarks a $3,000 top-of-the-line MacBook Pro in single-core CPU performance. That's bananas when you think about it.
If you're going to switch, switch.
You might as well tell them you're 'switching from gizmos to widgets under the hood'.
Basically, Apple likely wants ARM Mac developer transition hardware to be practical but boring. iPad Pros running MacOS would be neither.
The whole notion just doesn't feel like something Apple would do — it feels like something some users would like Apple to do.
It feels wrong, for example, to run an OS without touchscreen support on a touchscreen tablet.
Our current hardware is great and will be supported for many years to come; we're making this transition for the future.
As weird as 'MacBook' sounded at first, in hindsight it seems odd that until 2006 there had never been a Mac laptop with 'Mac' in its name.
key_insight: Apple's ARM transition will follow the same playbook as the 2005 Intel switch — pre-announced for developer readiness, driven by performance-per-watt superiority, and ultimately affecting the entire Mac lineup because half-measures don't make strategic sense.
